en.extract:
The puaiohi (Myadestes palmeri) or small Kauaʻi thrush is a rare species of songbird in the thrush family, Turdidae, that is endemic to the Hawaiian island of Kauaʻi. It is closely related to the other three endemic Hawaiian thrushes, the kāmaʻo, olomaʻo, and ʻōmaʻo.  It was first collected by Henry Palmer in 1891 at Halemanu around the entrance to the Kōkeʻe State Park.


Description
The plumage is mostly nondescript, with slaty-brown upperparts and a light gray breast and belly below. Birds have a black bill and pinkish feet.  A white eye ring is also fairly prominent and helps distinguish this bird from the other Hawaiian thrushes. Males and females are highly similar in appearance.  Juveniles show a pattern transitioning from a spotted whitish-buff above to a scalloped gray-brown below.


Distribution and habitat
Historically, this species has always been considered rare, favoring forested ravines above 1,050 metres (3,440 ft).  Puaiohi are restricted to the center and southern parts of the Alakaʻi Wilderness Preserve on the Hawaiian island of Kauaʻi. Seventy-five percent of the breeding population occurs in only 10 square kilometres (3.9 sq mi) of forest.


Diet and behavior
Outside the breeding season, most (82%) of the diet is fruit and berries, the remainder being insects and other invertebrates.  Important food sources include fruits of the native ʻolapa (Cheirodendron trigynum), lapalapa (C. platyphyllum), ʻōhiʻa ha (Syzygium sandwicensis) and kanawao (Broussaisia arguta).  In the breeding season, over fifty percent of the diet shifts to invertebrates.  The song is varied, consisting of a simple trill to a complex wheezing, and high-pitched squeal described as a squeaking rather resembling a metal wheel needing lubrication. Males sing throughout the year, but do so with increasing frequency as the breeding season approaches, peaking from April to May. Nesting has been recorded from as early as March to as late as mid-September. Nests are built in cavities or ledges of cliff faces, concealed by mosses and ferns, but tree cavities are also used.  Females are the sole nest builders, and nest building can take up to seven days. Females also incubate the eggs and broods and feeds the nestlings.  Eggs (usually two per clutch) are grayish-green to greenish-blue with irregular reddish-brown splotches.  Eggs hatch after 13–15 days.  After fledging, the male becomes the primary food provider to the young, while the female attempts a second brood.  Females will also attempt to re-nest if the first attempt fails.


Status and protection
According to recent data, population estimates range from 414 to 580 birds, and have remained somewhat stable since 1973, although a study published in 1986 estimated a population of approximately 100-125 birds.   Puaiohi populations are vulnerable to drought, hurricanes, and mammalian predation of both eggs and young.  Avian malaria also affects many birds, but a few birds have shown some resistance. (C. Atkinson, USGS, unpublished data). Feral pigs and goats also negatively affect populations of birds by degrading habitat, as has competition from many invasive plants and animals. The puaiohi was added to the United States Federal Endangered Species List on March 11, 1967.
In 1995, a captive breeding program was established.  Some birds from this program were being taken back to the Alakaʻi to supplement the wild population, though this program has been liquidated and released after inbreeding depression was observed in the captive population.


References


External links
Audubon Watchlist Species Account
USFWS Threatened and Endangered Animals in the Hawaiian Islands Species Account

The '''puaiohi''' ('''''Myadestes palmeri''''') or '''small Kaua{{okina}}i thrush''' is a rare species of [[songbird]] in the [[Thrush (bird)|thrush]] family, [[Turdidae]], that is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Hawaii]]an island of [[Kaua{{okina}}i]]. It is closely related to the other three endemic Hawaiian thrushes, the [[Kāmaʻo|kāma{{okina}}o]], [[Olomaʻo|oloma{{okina}}o]], and [[ʻŌmaʻo|{{okina}}ōma{{okina}}o]].  It was first collected by Henry Palmer in 1891 at Halemanu around the entrance to the [[Kōkeʻe State Park|Kōke{{okina}}e State Park]].<ref name=Berger>{{cite book | last = Berger | first = Andrew J. | title = Hawaiian Birdlife | edition = 2nd | year = 1983 | page = 98 | isbn = 0-8248-0742-1 | publisher = University Press of Hawaii | location = Honolulu}}</ref>

==Description==
The plumage is mostly nondescript, with [[slaty]]-brown upperparts and a light gray breast and belly below. Birds have a black bill and pinkish feet.  A white eye ring is also fairly prominent and helps distinguish this bird from the other Hawaiian thrushes. Males and females are highly similar in appearance.  Juveniles show a pattern transitioning from a spotted whitish-buff above to a scalloped gray-brown below.<ref name=HBW >Collar, N. J. (2005). Puaiohi (''Myadestes palmeri''). Pp 628-629 in: del Hoyo, J., Elliott, A. & Christie, D. A. eds. (2005). ''Handbook of the Birds of the World.'' Vol. 10. Cuckoo-shrikes to Thrushes. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona. {{ISBN|84-87334-72-5}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
Historically, this species has always been considered rare,<ref>Perkins, R. C. L. (1903). Vertebrata. pp. 365-466 in D. Sharp. (editor) Fauna Hawaiiensis. Vol. 1, part IV.  The University Press, Cambridge, U.K.</ref> favoring forested ravines above {{convert|1050|m|ft}}.  Puaiohi are restricted to the center and southern parts of the [[Alakai Wilderness Preserve|Alaka{{okina}}i Wilderness Preserve]] on the Hawaiian island of [[Kauaʻi]].<ref name=Hawaii>{{cite web|url=http://www.fws.gov/pacific/ecoservices/endangered/recovery/documents/HawaiiForestBirdsDraftRevisedRecoveryPlan.pdf|author=U.S. Fish|author2=Wildlife Service, Reg. 1|name-list-style=amp|title=Draft Revised Recovery Plan for Hawaiian Forest Birds, pg 36|access-date=2008-08-01}}</ref> Seventy-five percent of the breeding population occurs in only {{convert|10|km2|sqmi}} of forest.

==Diet and behavior==
Outside the breeding season, most (82%) of the diet is [[fruit]] and [[Berry|berries]], the remainder being [[insect]]s and other [[invertebrate]]s.<ref name= "Richardson and Bowles">{{cite journal|last=Richardson and Bowles |first=F. an J. |year= 1961 |title= Records of the rare forest birds of Kauaʻi, Hawaii|journal= Condor|volume= 63|pages=179–180|doi=10.2307/1365529|jstor=1365529 }}</ref>  Important food sources include fruits of the native {{okina}}olapa (''[[Cheirodendron trigynum]]''), lapalapa (''[[Cheirodendron platyphyllum|C. platyphyllum]]''), {{okina}}ōhi{{okina}}a ha (''[[Syzygium sandwicensis]]'') and kanawao (''[[Broussaisia|Broussaisia arguta]]'').  In the breeding season, over fifty percent of the diet shifts to invertebrates.<ref name=Hawaii2>{{cite web|url=http://www.fws.gov/pacific/ecoservices/endangered/recovery/documents/HawaiiForestBirdsDraftRevisedRecoveryPlan.pdf|author=U.S. Fish|author2=Wildlife Service, Reg. 1|name-list-style=amp|title=Draft Revised Recovery Plan for Hawaiian Forest Birds, pg. 37|access-date=2008-08-01}}</ref>  The song is varied, consisting of a simple trill to a complex wheezing, and high-pitched squeal described as a squeaking rather resembling a metal wheel needing lubrication.<ref name= Pratt >{{cite thesis| last = Pratt | first = H.D. | title = A systematic analysis of the endemic avifauna of the Hawaiian Islands|degree=PhD |publisher= Louisiana State University, Baton Rouge| year =1979}}</ref> Males sing throughout the year, but do so with increasing frequency as the breeding season approaches, peaking from April to May.<ref name=Hawaii/> Nesting has been recorded from as early as March to as late as mid-September. Nests are built in cavities or ledges of [[cliff]] faces, concealed by [[moss]]es and [[fern]]s,<ref name= "Kepler and Kepler">{{cite journal|last1=Kepler|first1=Cameron B. |last2=Kepler |first2= Angela K. |year= 1983 |title= A first record of the nest and chicks of the small Kauaʻi thrush|journal= Condor|volume= 85|issue= 4|pages=497–499|doi=10.2307/1367996|jstor=1367996 |bibcode=1983Condo..85..497K }}</ref> but tree cavities are also used.<ref name="Snetsinger 1999">{{cite book|vauthors=Snetsinger et al. |year=1999|chapter=  No. 461 Puaiohi (''Myadestes palmeri'')|title= The Birds of North America |editor=A. Poole |editor2= F. Gill |publisher= The Birds of North America, Inc.|location= Philadelphia, PA}}</ref>  Females are the sole nest builders, and nest building can take up to seven days.<ref name=Hawaii2/> Females also incubate the eggs and broods and feeds the nestlings.  Eggs (usually two per clutch) are grayish-green to greenish-blue with irregular reddish-brown splotches.<ref name=BergerA >{{cite book | last = Berger | first = Andrew J. | title = Hawaiian Birdlife | year = 1972 | isbn =978-0-8248-0213-4|publisher=University Press of Hawaii }}</ref>  Eggs hatch after 13–15 days.  After fledging, the male becomes the primary food provider to the young, while the female attempts a second brood.<ref name=Hawaii2/>  Females will also attempt to re-nest if the first attempt fails.

==Status and protection==
According to recent data, population estimates range from 414 to 580 birds, and have remained somewhat stable since 1973, although a study published in 1986 estimated a population of approximately 100-125 birds.<ref name="Snetsinger 1999"/>   Puaiohi populations are vulnerable to [[drought]], [[hurricanes]], and mammalian predation of both eggs and young.  [[Avian malaria]] also affects many birds, but a few birds have shown some resistance. (C. Atkinson, USGS, unpublished data). Feral pigs and goats also negatively affect populations of birds by degrading habitat, as has competition from many [[invasive plants]] and animals. The puaiohi was added to the United States Federal Endangered Species List on March 11, 1967.<ref name=USFWSendang>{{cite web|url=http://ecos.fws.gov/speciesProfile/SpeciesReport.do?spcode=B00S|author=U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service|title=Puaiohi-Species Profile|access-date=2008-08-01}}</ref>
In 1995, a captive breeding program was established.<ref>{{cite web|last=Roberts|first=Pauline|url=https://hawaii.conference-services.net/viewPDF.asp?conferenceID=1232&abstractID=238553|title=Measuring Success:Lessons Learned from the Puiaohi (''Myadestes palmeri'')|access-date=July 31, 2008 |display-authors=etal}}</ref>  Some birds from this program were being taken back to the [[Alakai Wilderness Preserve|Alaka{{okina}}i]] to supplement the wild population, though this program has been liquidated and released after [[inbreeding depression]] was observed in the captive population.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref name="Birdlife">{{cite web |author=Birdlife International |title=Puaiohi-Birdlife Species Factsheet |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/puaiohi-myadestes-palmeri |access-date=14 March 2025}}</ref>
